1: Psalm 99, the last of the YHWH-malak enthronement cluster, is distinguished by its triple "holy" refrain (qādôš hûʾ, holy is he) in vv. 3, 5, and 9 — the only psalm in the Psalter with this trisagion structure. The trisagion of Isa 6:3 ("holy, holy, holy is the LORD Almighty") echoes through this psalm, and Rev 4:8 takes up the triple "holy" in the heavenly worship. "He is enthroned above the cherubim (yōšēb kĕrûbîm)" — the ark-throne theology: the two golden cherubim on the ark's cover (kappōret) formed the divine throne-seat in the Most Holy Place (Exod 25:18-22; 1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2; 2 Kgs 19:15; Isa 37:16). YHWH's enthronement "above the cherubim" places the entire liturgy in the context of the sanctuary's Most Holy Place.
2: "The LORD is great (gādôl YHWH) in Zion; he is raised high above all the peoples" — the spatial and theological center: Zion is the place where YHWH's greatness is declared and experienced. Unlike Ps 97's cosmic theophany (mountains melting, lightnings flashing), Ps 99 localizes the divine presence in the specific sanctuary of Zion. This is the tension maintained throughout the Psalter: YHWH whose glory fills the heavens and the earth has chosen to dwell in a specific place — not because his presence is limited but because his covenant-condescension makes him accessible at a specific address.
3: "Let them praise your great and fearsome name (šimkā gādôl wĕnôrāʾ) — holy is he!" — the first occurrence of the trisagion. The divine name is the object of praise because the name carries the weight of the divine character and all divine acts. Nôrāʾ (fearsome, awe-inspiring, terrifying) — the root yārēʾ (to fear, to revere): the divine name inspires reverential awe, not just intellectual acknowledgment. "Holy is he (qādôš hûʾ)" — qādōš (holy, separate, set apart, the altogether Other) is the central divine attribute that all others serve. The triple repetition of "holy" at structurally pivotal points (vv. 3, 5, 9) makes holiness the interpretive key for the entire psalm.
4: "The mighty king loves justice (ûʿōz melek mišpāṭ ʾāhēb). You have established fairness; you have carried out justice and righteousness in Jacob" — the moral character of YHWH's kingship. Unlike ANE monarchies where divine kingship was often associated with arbitrary power, YHWH's kingship is constitutively just. "Loves justice (ʾāhēb mišpāṭ)" — divine love directed toward the right-ordering of community relationships. Isa 61:8: "I, the LORD, love justice; I hate robbery and wrongdoing." The double naming — "justice and righteousness in Jacob" (mišpāṭ ûṣĕdāqāh bĕyaʿăqōb) — is the prophetic pairing that defines the ethical ideal (Isa 1:27; 5:16; 9:7; 28:17; 33:5; Amos 5:7, 24; Mic 6:8).
5: "Exalt the LORD our God and bow down (wĕhištaḥăwû) at his footstool (lahădōm raglāyw) — holy is he!" — the second occurrence of the trisagion. The footstool of YHWH in the Psalter refers variously to the ark (1 Chr 28:2), the temple (Ps 132:7; Isa 66:1), or the earth (Isa 66:1). To bow at the footstool is to prostrate before the enthroned king in the most submissive posture available. In ANE royal iconography, conquered enemies were depicted under the king's feet — prostration at the footstool was the act of subjects acknowledging sovereignty. Ps 110:1 ("until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet") uses the footstool in this conquest register; here the footstool is the place of voluntary worship.
6: "Moses and Aaron were among his priests; Samuel was among those who called on his name. They called out to the LORD, and he answered them" — a historical survey of Israel's great intercessors, deployed as evidence for YHWH's responsiveness to prayer. Moses and Aaron were not technically kōhănîm (priests from Levi's line through Aaron) in the narrow sense, but the psalm uses the term broadly for those who served in mediatorial roles before YHWH. Samuel was not of the Levitical priesthood but served at Shiloh (1 Sam 1-3) and was renowned as a powerful intercessor (1 Sam 7:5-9; 12:19-23; Jer 15:1). The naming of three covenant intercessors establishes that YHWH is the God who hears prayer — not a distant deity but one who answers when called upon through designated mediators.
7: "He spoke to them in the pillar of cloud (bĕʿammûd ʿānān); they kept his testimonies (ʿēdōṯāyw) and the law (wĕḥōq) he gave them" — the wilderness theophany extended through the priestly/prophetic mediation of Moses, Aaron, and Samuel. The pillar of cloud as divine speech-medium (Exod 33:9-11; Num 12:5-8; Deut 31:15) connects the divine address in the wilderness to the ongoing divine speaking through the designated mediators. "They kept his testimonies" — the ʿēdôṯ (testimonies, the covenant stipulations deposited in the ark — Exod 25:16, 21; 31:18) were specifically kept by the priestly guardians. The intercessors were simultaneously obedient keepers of the covenant requirements.
8: "LORD our God, you answered them; you were a God who forgave them (ʾēl nōśēʾ lāhem), yet you held them accountable (nōqēm) for their offenses" — the theological tension of divine governance: simultaneous forgiveness and accountability. Nāśāʾ (to lift, to carry, to forgive) — sin lifted and carried away; the same verb as in the scapegoat ritual (Lev 16:22: "the goat will carry on itself all their iniquities"). Nāqam (to avenge, to hold accountable) — the same God who forgives also ensures that sin's consequences are not eliminated. The verse holds both without resolving the tension into a single principle: YHWH both forgave Moses and held him accountable for striking the rock (Num 20:10-12). The NT resolution: both the forgiveness and the accountability are absorbed by Christ on the cross — he forgives because he bears the consequence.
9: "Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain (har qodšô), for the LORD our God is holy!" — the third and final occurrence of the trisagion, now with "at his holy mountain" replacing "at his footstool" (v5). The escalating specificity — footstool (v5) → holy mountain (v9) — moves from general submission to the specific cultic location. Zion/Jerusalem as YHWH's holy mountain (Pss 2:6; 48:1-2; 87:1; Isa 2:2-4; Zech 8:3; Rev 21:10) is the geographic point where heaven and earth meet. The triple "holy" creates a trisagion frame for the entire psalm, making holiness the Alpha and Omega of the proclamation of YHWH's kingship.
